original_hypothesis.title: Cross-Chain Wallet Bricking via Upgrade Replay to Non-Existent Implementation
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The canSkipChainIdValidation function whitelists upgradeToAndCall.selector for cross-chain replay. A UserOp performing a UUPS upgrade via executeWithoutChainIdValidation is signed without chain ID binding. After an upgrade is executed on chain A, anyone can observe the on-chain transaction and replay the identical UserOp on chain B. If the new implementation address does not have deployed code on chain B (common when deployments are staggered across chains), the wallet proxy points to empty code, permanently bricking the wallet and locking all funds on chain B.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The upgrade whitelist is an explicit design choice, but the asymmetric deployment risk across chains creates a window where legitimate upgrades become attack vectors. This is not hypothetical - cross-chain deployment ordering is a common operational reality.

proof_of_signal.pattern: canSkipChainIdValidation returns true for UUPSUpgradeable.upgradeToAndCall.selector, enabling cross-chain replay of implementation upgrades

preconditions: Wallet exists on multiple chains at the same address
Owner performs a UUPS upgrade via executeWithoutChainIdValidation on chain A
New implementation address has no code on chain B
Attacker observes the upgrade transaction on chain A (on-chain, not mempool)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: upgradeToAndCall whitelisted at line 232 alongside owner management functions
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires wallet owner to perform a legitimate upgrade on any chain
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Time between upgrade on chain A and implementation deployment on chain B

operational_questions: Is there a deployment procedure ensuring simultaneous implementation deployment across all target chains?
What happens to the proxy when implementation has no code - is it permanently bricked?
Can the upgrade be reversed on chain B if the implementation is later deployed?
validation_requirements: Test UUPS upgrade replay to an address with no code
Verify proxy behavior after upgrade to empty implementation
Confirm whether wallet can be recovered after bricking

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor chain A for wallet upgrade transactions using executeWithoutChainIdValidation
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Extract the signed UserOp from the on-chain transaction
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Check if the new implementation exists on chain B
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: If not deployed on chain B, submit the same UserOp on chain B to brick the wallet
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Only on-chain observation needed; no victim cooperation required

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete wallet takeover if malicious code at target address; permanent bricking if no code
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All wallet operations on chain B permanently disabled
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No recovery possible if proxy points to empty implementation; hard fork needed
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All wallet functions on the affected chain

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Moderate - understanding of UUPS and cross-chain replay mechanics
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Window between upgrade on chain A and deployment on chain B

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: upgradeToAndCall UserOps using REPLAYABLE_NONCE_KEY on any chain
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Implementation addresses that don't exist on all target chains

validation_result.mechanism: cross-chain upgrade replay to non-existent/different implementation
validation_result.impact_summary: The whitelisting of upgradeToAndCall in canSkipChainIdValidation allows cross-chain replay of UUPS upgrades. If the target implementation lacks code on chain B (or contains different code), the wallet can be permanently bricked or compromised, locking all funds.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The code at CoinbaseSmartWallet.sol:226-234 explicitly whitelists `UUPSUpgradeable.upgradeToAndCall.selector` for cross-chain replay via `canSkipChainIdValidation`. When an owner performs an upgrade through `executeWithoutChainIdValidation`, the UserOp hash is computed without chain ID binding (line 148: `getUserOpHashWithoutChainId`), making the identical signed UserOp valid on all chains where the wallet exists. An attacker observing the upgrade tx on chain A can extract the UserOp and submit it on chain B. If the new implementation address has no deployed code on chain B, the proxy's fallback will delegatecall to empty code, which succeeds with empty return data - rendering all wallet functions non-functional. The upgrade function itself becomes inoperable because the implementation code no longer exists behind the proxy, making this permanent. Additionally, `_authorizeUpgrade` (line 309) contains only an `onlyOwner` check with zero validation of the new implementation address (no code existence check, no ERC-1822 compliance check). Even if Solady's internal `upgradeToAndCall` were to revert on codeless addresses, an attacker who deploys different/malicious code at the same address on chain B via CREATE2 could hijack the wallet entirely.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 226-234 of CoinbaseSmartWallet.sol conclusively show `upgradeToAndCall.selector` in the cross-chain replay whitelist. Lines 180-187 show `executeWithoutChainIdValidation` performs a self-call with the whitelisted selector. Lines 147-149 show the hash is recomputed without chain ID. Line 309 shows `_authorizeUpgrade` performs no implementation validation. The complete chain from signature validation through execution is chain-ID-independent for this operation.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: upgradeToAndCall.selector is whitelisted for cross-chain replay. An upgrade operation signed for any chain can be replayed on every other chain where the wallet exists, with no safeguard ensuring the target implementation address has identical safe code on all chains.
Executes whitelisted selectors as a self-call. When the data contains upgradeToAndCall calldata, it performs the UUPS upgrade without any chain-specific validation of the implementation address.
When the call targets executeWithoutChainIdValidation, the userOpHash is recomputed without chain ID, making the exact same signed UserOp valid on every chain.

vulnerable_code_snippets.code_snippet: function _authorizeUpgrade(address) internal view virtual override(UUPSUpgradeable) onlyOwner {}
vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Empty authorization body - only checks ownership via onlyOwner modifier, performs no validation on the new implementation address (no code existence check, no ERC-1822 proxiableUUID check).

calling_sequence: 1. Owner signs UserOp targeting executeWithoutChainIdValidation with upgradeToAndCall(newImpl, data) payload on chain A, using REPLAYABLE_NONCE_KEY
2. EntryPoint on chain A calls validateUserOp -> hash recomputed via getUserOpHashWithoutChainId (no chain ID) -> signature valid
3. EntryPoint calls executeWithoutChainIdValidation -> selector passes canSkipChainIdValidation -> _call(address(this), 0, data) executes upgradeToAndCall
4. upgradeToAndCall calls _authorizeUpgrade (passes - msg.sender == address(this)) -> implementation slot updated to newImpl
5. Attacker observes chain A transaction, extracts the complete signed UserOp
6. Attacker submits identical UserOp on chain B where newImpl has no code (or different code)
7. EntryPoint on chain B: validateUserOp -> same chain-ID-free hash -> same signature valid -> same nonce key valid
8. EntryPoint calls executeWithoutChainIdValidation -> upgradeToAndCall sets implementation to newImpl on chain B
9. Wallet on chain B now points to empty/malicious implementation - permanently bricked or compromised
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The complete path from UserOp validation through execution is chain-ID-independent when using executeWithoutChainIdValidation. The hash computed at CoinbaseSmartWallet.sol:148 (getUserOpHashWithoutChainId) omits chain ID. The selector check at line 183 passes for upgradeToAndCall (line 232). The self-call at line 186 triggers the proxy's fallback -> delegatecall to implementation -> upgradeToAndCall stores new implementation address. No step in this path validates that the new implementation has code or is safe on the current chain.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided. The foundry.toml shows standard configuration with no chain-specific deployment logic.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is realistic and requires only on-chain observation. After a legitimate upgrade on chain A: (1) The signed UserOp is publicly visible on-chain. (2) The attacker extracts it and checks if newImpl has code on chain B. (3) If no code exists (common during staggered multi-chain deployments), the attacker submits the UserOp to a bundler on chain B. (4) The nonce must match - since replayable operations are designed to sync across chains, nonce alignment is expected by design. (5) The wallet on chain B is upgraded to point to empty code, permanently bricking it. The attack window exists between the upgrade on chain A and deployment of the implementation on chain B.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL. If the implementation has no code on chain B, the wallet proxy delegates to empty code, making all functions return empty data. The wallet cannot execute transactions, cannot upgrade further, and cannot recover. All ETH, ERC-20 tokens, and NFTs held by the wallet on chain B are permanently locked with no recovery path short of a chain hard fork. If an attacker deploys malicious code at the implementation address on chain B via CREATE2, they gain full control of the wallet and can drain all assets.
recommended_mitigations: Remove UUPSUpgradeable.upgradeToAndCall.selector from the canSkipChainIdValidation whitelist, requiring upgrades to be chain-specific operations
If cross-chain upgrade replay is desired, add implementation validation in _authorizeUpgrade: require(newImplementation.code.length > 0) and verify ERC-1822 proxiableUUID
Add a timelock or multi-step upgrade process that allows owners to cancel a pending upgrade on any chain before it takes effect
Consider adding a rescue mechanism that allows recovery if the implementation becomes invalid (e.g., a fallback owner stored in proxy storage, not implementation storage)
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy CoinbaseSmartWallet on two test chains (Anvil forks) with same address via factory. 2. Deploy a valid new implementation on chain A only. 3. On chain A, submit a UserOp via EntryPoint that calls executeWithoutChainIdValidation with upgradeToAndCall(newImpl, '') using REPLAYABLE_NONCE_KEY. 4. Extract the signed UserOp from chain A's transaction. 5. Verify newImpl has no code on chain B. 6. Submit the identical UserOp on chain B. 7. Verify wallet on chain B has implementation set to newImpl (empty code). 8. Attempt any wallet operation on chain B and confirm it fails/returns empty data. 9. Attempt to upgrade wallet on chain B and confirm it's impossible.
uncertainties: Solady's UUPSUpgradeable.upgradeToAndCall may contain an internal code existence check for the new implementation - this would prevent bricking but NOT prevent compromise if different code exists at the address
Exact behavior of proxy delegatecall to codeless address may vary by EVM implementation (though EVM spec says it returns success with empty data)
Nonce synchronization across chains depends on the user having performed the same sequence of replayable operations on both chains
additional_testing_needed: Verify Solady UUPSUpgradeable.upgradeToAndCall behavior when target has no code
Test proxy behavior after implementation set to codeless address (can any function be called?)
Test whether CREATE2 can be used to deploy different code at the same address on different chains
Verify EntryPoint nonce management for REPLAYABLE_NONCE_KEY across chains
